rawText:
Trust is one of those things that takes a long time to build and very little time to destroy, and nearly everyone has experienced both sides of that asymmetry.

The reason trust breaks so easily is rooted in how the human brain processes risk: negative experiences carry more psychological weight than positive ones, a phenomenon researchers call "negativity bias."

A colleague who delivers excellent work for two years and then misses one critical deadline will be remembered primarily for the failure, not for the years of reliability that preceded it.

This imbalance means that restoring trust after a betrayal or a serious mistake requires far more effort than maintaining it would have — and the path back is neither straightforward nor guaranteed.

The first obstacle is that the person who broke the trust often underestimates the damage, while the person who was harmed tends to overestimate it.

This perception gap creates frustration on both sides: one party feels that the reaction is excessive, and the other feels that the apology is insufficient.

Genuine repair begins with acknowledgment — not a vague expression of regret, but a specific recognition of what was done, why it was harmful, and what the consequences have been for the other person.

Without that specificity, an apology can feel like a performance designed to move past the incident rather than to address it honestly.

The next step is consistent behavior over time, because trust is rebuilt through patterns, not through single gestures.

A grand apology followed by unchanged behavior is worse than no apology at all, because it adds the wound of broken promises to the original harm.

Conceding fault is particularly difficult in organizational settings, where admitting error can carry legal or reputational risks, yet studies consistently show that institutions that acknowledge mistakes transparently recover public trust faster than those that deny or deflect.

The paradox is that the very act of admitting vulnerability — saying "we were wrong and here is what we are doing about it" — signals the kind of honesty that trust depends on.

Rebuilding trust also requires patience from the injured party, who must be willing to observe new behavior long enough to determine whether it represents a genuine change or merely a temporary adjustment.

Neither side can rush the process, because trust restored too quickly is fragile, while trust rebuilt gradually through demonstrated reliability becomes, in many cases, even stronger than what existed before.

overview.title: Why Trust Is Hard to Rebuild

sentences.text: Trust takes a long time to build, but it can break very quickly.
sentences.translation: 신뢰는 쌓는 데 오래 걸리지만, 무너지는 건 아주 빨라요.

sentences.text: Our brains remember bad things more than good things, so one mistake feels really big.
sentences.translation: 우리 뇌는 좋은 일보다 나쁜 일을 더 잘 기억해서, 실수 하나가 아주 크게 느껴져요.

sentences.text: If you hurt someone, saying sorry once is not enough — you have to show you changed.
sentences.translation: 누군가에게 상처를 주면, 한 번 미안하다고 말하는 것만으로는 부족해요 — 변했다는 걸 보여줘야 해요.

sentences.text: A good apology means explaining exactly what you did wrong and why it was hurtful.
sentences.translation: 좋은 사과는 정확히 무엇을 잘못했고 왜 그게 상처가 되었는지 설명하는 거예요.

sentences.text: Big companies also lose trust, and the ones that admit mistakes honestly get trust back faster.
sentences.translation: 큰 회사들도 신뢰를 잃는데, 실수를 솔직히 인정하는 회사가 신뢰를 더 빨리 되찾아요.

sentences.text: Getting trust back takes time and patience from both people, but it can become even stronger than before.
sentences.translation: 신뢰를 되찾으려면 양쪽 모두 시간과 인내가 필요하지만, 이전보다 더 강해질 수도 있어요.

sentences.text: The article explores why trust is easy to destroy but extremely difficult to restore.
sentences.translation: 이 글은 신뢰가 왜 무너지기는 쉽지만 회복하기는 극히 어려운지를 탐구해요.

sentences.text: Human brains are wired to weigh negative experiences more heavily than positive ones, which is called negativity bias.
sentences.translation: 인간의 뇌는 긍정적 경험보다 부정적 경험에 더 큰 비중을 두도록 설계되어 있는데, 이를 부정성 편향이라고 해요.

sentences.text: True repair requires a specific acknowledgment of wrongdoing followed by consistent changed behavior over time.
sentences.translation: 진정한 회복에는 잘못에 대한 구체적 인정과 시간에 걸친 일관된 행동 변화가 필요해요.

sentences.text: A perception gap between the offender and the victim often makes rebuilding trust even harder.
sentences.translation: 가해자와 피해자 사이의 인식 차이가 신뢰 재건을 더욱 어렵게 만들어요.

sentences.text: Organizations that transparently admit mistakes recover public trust faster than those that deny or deflect blame.
sentences.translation: 실수를 투명하게 인정하는 조직이 부인하거나 책임을 회피하는 조직보다 공적 신뢰를 더 빨리 회복해요.

sentences.text: Trust rebuilt gradually through demonstrated reliability can end up stronger than the original bond.
sentences.translation: 입증된 신뢰성을 통해 점진적으로 재건된 신뢰는 원래의 유대보다 더 강해질 수 있어요.

sentences.text: This analytical essay uses psychological research — particularly negativity bias — to explain the structural asymmetry between building and destroying trust.
sentences.translation: 이 분석적 에세이는 심리학 연구, 특히 부정성 편향을 활용해 신뢰를 쌓는 것과 무너뜨리는 것 사이의 구조적 비대칭성을 설명해요.

sentences.text: The writer employs a cause-and-effect framework, moving from cognitive science to interpersonal dynamics to organizational behavior.
sentences.translation: 글쓴이는 인지과학에서 대인 관계 역학, 조직 행동으로 이어지는 인과관계 체계를 활용해요.

sentences.text: Abstract nouns like "asymmetry," "vulnerability," and "reliability" carry the argumentative weight, requiring readers to process concept-dense prose.
sentences.translation: "비대칭성", "취약성", "신뢰성" 같은 추상 명사가 논증의 무게를 지탱하며, 독자에게 개념 밀도가 높은 문장 처리를 요구해요.

sentences.text: Paradox serves as a key rhetorical device: admitting weakness becomes the very signal of strength that trust requires.
sentences.translation: 역설이 핵심 수사 장치로 작동해요: 약함을 인정하는 것이 바로 신뢰가 요구하는 강함의 신호가 되는 거예요.

sentences.text: The essay balances prescriptive claims with hedging language — "neither straightforward nor guaranteed," "in many cases" — maintaining academic nuance.
sentences.translation: 이 에세이는 처방적 주장을 "직접적이지도 보장되지도 않는", "많은 경우에" 같은 완화 표현으로 균형을 잡아 학술적 뉘앙스를 유지해요.

sentences.text: The concluding sentence resolves the tension by suggesting that trust rebuilt through effort may surpass the original, turning loss into potential gain.
sentences.translation: 결론 문장은 노력으로 재건된 신뢰가 원래 것을 넘어설 수 있다는 제안으로 긴장을 해소하며, 상실을 잠재적 이득으로 전환해요.

sentences.original: Trust is one of those things that takes a long time to build and very little time to destroy, and nearly everyone has experienced both sides of that asymmetry.
sentences.translation: 신뢰는 쌓는 데 오랜 시간이 걸리고 무너뜨리는 데는 아주 짧은 시간이 걸리는 것 중 하나이며, 거의 모든 사람이 그 비대칭성의 양면을 경험해 본 적이 있다.

grammarElements.text: one of those things that takes a long time to build and very little time to destroy
grammarElements.label: 보어 (관계절 포함 명사구)

grammarElements.text: nearly everyone has experienced both sides of that asymmetry
grammarElements.label: 병렬절 (현재완료)

vocabulary.example: Trust is the foundation of any relationship.
One lie can destroy years of trust.
There is an asymmetry between effort and reward.

expressions.usage: 보편적으로 인정되는 사실을 자연스럽게 도입할 때 쓰는 표현
어떤 현상의 두 가지 측면을 모두 경험했음을 나타낼 때 쓴다

patterns.pattern: [Abstract noun] is one of those things that [takes time to A] and [very little time to B]
patterns.explanation: 추상 개념의 비대칭적 특성을 대조적으로 제시하며 글을 여는 패턴. "a long time"과 "very little time"의 대비가 핵심이다.
examples: Reputation is one of those things that takes years to earn and seconds to ruin.
Confidence is one of those things that takes practice to build and one failure to shake.

drillQuestions.question: Trust takes a long time to build and very little time to _____.
"Asymmetry" means a lack of _____.
Nearly everyone _____ experienced both sides of that asymmetry.
"One of those things that" introduces a _____ truth.

sentences.notes: 글의 도입부로, 추상 명사(Trust)를 주어로 놓고 대조 구조(a long time vs. very little time)로 핵심 주제를 즉시 제시한다. "asymmetry"라는 고급 어휘가 글의 분석적 성격을 예고한다.

koreanAlignment.en: Trust is one of those things
that takes a long time to build
and very little time to destroy
koreanAlignment.ko: 무너뜨리는 데는 아주 짧은 시간이 걸리는

koreanAlignment.en: and nearly everyone has experienced
koreanAlignment.ko: 거의 모든 사람이 경험해 본 적이 있다

koreanAlignment.en: both sides of that asymmetry

pronunciationNotes: asymmetry: /eɪˈsɪmətri/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "에이시머트리"에 가깝다. a-가 /eɪ/로 발음됨에 주의.
destroy: /dɪˈstrɔɪ/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. str을 한 번에 빠르게 발음. "디스트로이"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: The reason trust breaks so easily is rooted in how the human brain processes risk: negative experiences carry more psychological weight than positive ones, a phenomenon researchers call "negativity bias."
sentences.translation: 신뢰가 그토록 쉽게 무너지는 이유는 인간의 뇌가 위험을 처리하는 방식에 뿌리를 두고 있다: 부정적 경험이 긍정적 경험보다 더 큰 심리적 비중을 차지하는데, 이를 연구자들은 "부정성 편향"이라 부른다.

grammarElements.text: The reason trust breaks so easily
grammarElements.label: 주어 (명사 + 관계절)

grammarElements.text: how the human brain processes risk
grammarElements.label: 보어 (간접의문문)

grammarElements.text: negative experiences carry more psychological weight than positive ones
grammarElements.label: 콜론 뒤 설명절

vocabulary.example: The problem is rooted in a lack of communication.
The trauma had a lasting psychological effect.
Negativity bias makes us focus on bad news.

expressions.usage: 어떤 현상의 근본 원인을 설명할 때 쓰는 격식적 표현
어떤 요소가 다른 것보다 영향력이 크다는 뜻

patterns.pattern: The reason [X] is rooted in [how/what]: [explanation], a phenomenon called [name]
patterns.explanation: 현상의 원인을 밝히고, 콜론 뒤에 설명을 덧붙인 다음, 동격구로 학술 용어를 소개하는 패턴.
examples: The reason people fear public speaking is rooted in how the brain perceives social threat: being judged activates the same circuits as physical danger, a response psychologists call "social anxiety."
The reason habits form quickly is rooted in how neural pathways strengthen: repeated actions become automatic, a process scientists call "myelination."

drillQuestions.question: The reason trust breaks easily is _____ in how the brain processes risk.
Negative experiences carry more psychological _____ than positive ones.
Researchers call this phenomenon "negativity _____."
"Is rooted in" means the cause is _____ connected to something.

sentences.notes: 콜론으로 이어지는 설명 구조와 동격구(a phenomenon researchers call ~)가 결합된 고급 문장이다. 학술 용어(negativity bias)를 자연스럽게 도입하며 글의 분석적 깊이를 더한다.

koreanAlignment.en: The reason trust breaks so easily
is rooted in how the human brain processes risk
koreanAlignment.ko: 인간의 뇌가 위험을 처리하는 방식에 뿌리를 두고 있다

koreanAlignment.en: negative experiences carry more psychological weight
koreanAlignment.ko: 부정적 경험이 더 큰 심리적 비중을 차지하는데

koreanAlignment.en: than positive ones
a phenomenon researchers call "negativity bias"

pronunciationNotes: psychological: /ˌsaɪkəˈlɑːdʒɪkəl/ — 세 번째 음절에 강세. p는 묵음. "사이컬라지컬"에 가깝다.
phenomenon: /fɪˈnɑːmɪnɑːn/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. ph를 /f/로 발음. "피나머넌"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: A colleague who delivers excellent work for two years and then misses one critical deadline will be remembered primarily for the failure, not for the years of reliability that preceded it.
sentences.translation: 2년간 훌륭한 업무 성과를 보여 주다가 중요한 마감을 한 번 놓친 동료는, 그 전의 수년간의 신뢰성이 아니라 주로 그 실패로 기억될 것이다.

grammarElements.text: A colleague who delivers excellent work for two years and then misses one critical deadline
grammarElements.label: 주어 (관계절 포함 긴 명사구)

grammarElements.text: not for the years of reliability that preceded it
grammarElements.label: 부정 전치사구 + 관계절

vocabulary.example: My colleague helped me finish the project.
Missing a critical deadline can ruin a project.
Her reliability made her a valued team member.
The calm that preceded the storm was eerie.

expressions.usage: 어떤 사람에 대한 핵심 인상을 말할 때 쓰는 표현
기대와 다른 기억 방식을 대비할 때 쓴다

patterns.pattern: [Person] who [positive action] and then [negative action] will be remembered for [the negative], not for [the positive]
patterns.explanation: 긍정적 행동과 부정적 행동을 대비시킨 뒤, 부정적 행동이 더 강하게 기억된다는 부정성 편향을 구체적 사례로 보여주는 패턴.
examples: A chef who cooks perfectly for months and then serves one bad dish will be remembered for the mistake, not for the months of excellence.
A driver who drives safely for years and then causes one accident will be remembered for the crash, not for the safe years.

drillQuestions.question: A colleague who delivers excellent work will be remembered primarily for the _____.
"The years of reliability that preceded it" means the reliability came _____ the failure.
"Will be remembered" is in the _____ passive voice.
The sentence illustrates the concept of negativity _____.

sentences.notes: 부정성 편향의 구체적 사례를 제시하는 문장. 관계절(who delivers ~ and then misses ~)이 긴 주어를 형성하고, "primarily for the failure, not for ~"의 대조 구조가 요점을 선명히 한다.

koreanAlignment.en: A colleague who delivers excellent work for two years
and then misses one critical deadline
koreanAlignment.ko: 중요한 마감을 한 번 놓친 동료는

koreanAlignment.en: will be remembered primarily for the failure
not for the years of reliability that preceded it

pronunciationNotes: colleague: /ˈkɑːliːɡ/ — 첫 음절에 강세. 두 음절로 발음. "칼리그"에 가깝다.
reliability: /rɪˌlaɪəˈbɪləti/ — 네 번째 음절에 강세. "릴라이어빌러티"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: This imbalance means that restoring trust after a betrayal or a serious mistake requires far more effort than maintaining it would have — and the path back is neither straightforward nor guaranteed.
sentences.translation: 이 불균형은 배신이나 심각한 실수 이후 신뢰를 회복하는 데는 유지하는 것보다 훨씬 더 많은 노력이 필요하다는 것을 의미한다 — 그리고 되돌아가는 길은 직접적이지도 보장되지도 않는다.

grammarElements.text: that restoring trust after a betrayal or a serious mistake requires far more effort than maintaining it would have
grammarElements.label: that 명사절 (동명사 주어 + 비교급)

grammarElements.text: and the path back is neither straightforward nor guaranteed
grammarElements.label: 병렬절 (neither ... nor)

vocabulary.example: The imbalance between effort and reward is frustrating.
A betrayal of trust is hard to forgive.
The solution is not as straightforward as it seems.
Success is never guaranteed.

expressions.usage: far가 비교급 more를 강조하여 격차가 크다는 것을 나타낸다
neither A nor B 구조로 두 가지를 동시에 부정하는 격식적 표현

patterns.pattern: This [abstract noun] means that [gerund] requires far more [noun] than [gerund] — and [consequence with neither ... nor]
patterns.explanation: 앞 문장의 개념을 받아 그 함의를 풀어내는 패턴. 동명사 주어로 비교하고, 대시 뒤에 neither ... nor로 부정적 전망을 덧붙인다.
examples: This gap means that repairing a relationship requires far more honesty than starting one — and the outcome is neither predictable nor painless.
This delay means that catching up later requires far more time than keeping pace — and the result is neither easy nor certain.

drillQuestions.question: Restoring trust requires far more _____ than maintaining it.
The path back is neither straightforward _____ guaranteed.
"This imbalance" refers to the imbalance between _____ and negative experiences.
"A betrayal" is an act of breaking someone's _____.

sentences.notes: 동명사 주어(restoring trust)와 비교 구문(far more ... than maintaining it would have)이 결합된 복잡한 구조이다. "neither ... nor"가 회복 과정의 불확실성을 강조한다. 대시(—)로 비관적 전망을 추가한다.

koreanAlignment.en: This imbalance means that
restoring trust after a betrayal or a serious mistake
koreanAlignment.ko: 배신이나 심각한 실수 이후 신뢰를 회복하는 데는

koreanAlignment.en: requires far more effort than maintaining it would have
koreanAlignment.ko: 유지하는 것보다 훨씬 더 많은 노력이 필요하다는 것을

koreanAlignment.en: and the path back is neither straightforward nor guaranteed
koreanAlignment.ko: 그리고 되돌아가는 길은 직접적이지도 보장되지도 않는다

pronunciationNotes: betrayal: /bɪˈtreɪəl/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "비트레이얼"에 가깝다.
guaranteed: /ˌɡærənˈtiːd/ — 세 번째 음절에 강세. "개런티드"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: The first obstacle is that the person who broke the trust often underestimates the damage, while the person who was harmed tends to overestimate it.
sentences.translation: 첫 번째 장애물은 신뢰를 깨뜨린 사람이 피해를 과소평가하는 경향이 있는 반면, 피해를 입은 사람은 과대평가하는 경향이 있다는 것이다.

grammarElements.text: that the person who broke the trust often underestimates the damage
grammarElements.label: that 보어절 (관계절 포함)

grammarElements.text: while the person who was harmed tends to overestimate it
grammarElements.label: while 대조절 (관계절 포함)

vocabulary.example: The first obstacle to change is denial.
People often underestimate the impact of their words.
Victims tend to overestimate the harm done to them.

expressions.phrase: the person who broke the trust
expressions.usage: 관계절로 가해자를 완곡하게 지칭하는 격식적 표현
tend to로 일반적 경향을 나타내는 학술적 표현

patterns.pattern: The first obstacle is that [A] often [verb A], while [B] tends to [verb B]
patterns.explanation: 양측의 인식 차이를 while로 대조하며, 신뢰 회복의 첫 번째 어려움을 구조적으로 제시하는 패턴.
examples: The first challenge is that managers often underestimate workloads, while employees tend to overreport them.
The first difficulty is that parents often simplify the issue, while children tend to dramatize it.

drillQuestions.question: The person who broke the trust often _____ the damage.
The person who was harmed tends to _____ the damage.
"While" in this sentence signals a _____.
"The first obstacle" means the first _____ to rebuilding trust.

sentences.notes: while절로 가해자와 피해자의 인식 차이를 대비시킨다. underestimates와 overestimate의 접두사 대조(under- vs. over-)가 인식의 간극을 효과적으로 표현한다.

koreanAlignment.en: The first obstacle is that
the person who broke the trust
often underestimates the damage
while the person who was harmed
tends to overestimate it

pronunciationNotes: obstacle: /ˈɑːbstəkəl/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "압스터클"에 가깝다. b 다음의 s를 확실히 발음.
underestimates: /ˌʌndərˈestɪmeɪts/ — 세 번째 음절에 강세. "언더레스티메이츠"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: This perception gap creates frustration on both sides: one party feels that the reaction is excessive, and the other feels that the apology is insufficient.
sentences.translation: 이 인식 차이는 양쪽 모두에게 좌절감을 만든다: 한쪽은 반응이 과도하다고 느끼고, 다른 쪽은 사과가 불충분하다고 느낀다.

grammarElements.text: one party feels that the reaction is excessive
grammarElements.label: 콜론 뒤 설명절 1

grammarElements.text: and the other feels that the apology is insufficient
grammarElements.label: 콜론 뒤 설명절 2 (병렬)

vocabulary.example: Perception of fairness differs between people.
The miscommunication led to frustration on both sides.
The punishment seemed excessive for such a small mistake.
The apology was insufficient to repair the damage.

expressions.usage: 양측의 이해나 판단이 다를 때 그 간극을 가리키는 학술적 표현
갈등의 두 당사자 모두가 영향을 받는다는 뜻

patterns.pattern: This [noun] gap creates [emotion] on both sides: one [party] feels [A], and the other feels [B]
patterns.explanation: 인식 차이로 인한 감정적 결과를 콜론 뒤에 양측의 관점으로 구체화하는 패턴.
examples: This expectation gap creates tension on both sides: one group feels overworked, and the other feels underappreciated.
This communication gap creates confusion on both sides: one person feels ignored, and the other feels overwhelmed.

drillQuestions.question: This perception gap creates _____ on both sides.
One party feels that the reaction is _____.
The other feels that the apology is _____.
"Perception gap" means a difference in how two sides _____ the situation.

sentences.notes: 콜론 뒤에 양측의 감정을 병렬로 배치해 인식 차이의 구조를 명확히 한다. excessive/insufficient의 형용사 대조가 감정적 간극을 선명하게 표현한다.

koreanAlignment.en: This perception gap
creates frustration on both sides
one party feels that the reaction is excessive
and the other feels that the apology is insufficient

pronunciationNotes: excessive: /ɪkˈsesɪv/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "익세시브"에 가깝다.
insufficient: /ˌɪnsəˈfɪʃənt/ — 세 번째 음절에 강세. "인서피션트"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: Genuine repair begins with acknowledgment — not a vague expression of regret, but a specific recognition of what was done, why it was harmful, and what the consequences have been for the other person.
sentences.translation: 진정한 회복은 인정에서 시작된다 — 막연한 후회의 표현이 아니라, 무엇을 했는지, 왜 그것이 해로웠는지, 그리고 상대방에게 어떤 결과를 초래했는지에 대한 구체적인 인식이다.

grammarElements.text: not a vague expression of regret
grammarElements.label: 부정 동격구

grammarElements.text: but a specific recognition of what was done, why it was harmful, and what the consequences have been for the other person
grammarElements.label: 긍정 동격구 (간접의문문 3개 병렬)

vocabulary.example: A genuine apology requires sincerity.
Acknowledgment of the mistake was the first step.
A vague apology feels dishonest.
Every action has consequences.

expressions.usage: 회복 과정의 첫 단계를 제시할 때 쓰는 표현

expressions.phrase: not a vague ... but a specific
expressions.usage: not A but B 구조로 올바른 방식과 잘못된 방식을 대비한다

patterns.pattern: [Process] begins with [noun] — not [vague version], but [specific version of what/why/what]
patterns.explanation: 회복 과정의 시작점을 명시하고, 대시 뒤에 "not A but B" 구조로 피상적 방식과 진정한 방식을 대비하는 패턴.
examples: Healing begins with honesty — not a generic statement of regret, but a detailed admission of what went wrong and who was affected.
Reform begins with accountability — not a brief press release, but a clear explanation of what happened and how it will be prevented.

drillQuestions.question: Genuine repair begins with _____.
Not a vague expression of regret, but a _____ recognition.
The sentence lists three things: what was done, why it was harmful, and what the _____ have been.
"Genuine repair" means repair that is _____.

sentences.notes: 대시(—) 뒤에 "not A, but B" 구조를 사용해 진정한 인정과 형식적 사과를 명확히 구분한다. 세 개의 간접의문문(what/why/what)이 병렬로 나열되어 구체성을 강조한다.

koreanAlignment.en: Genuine repair begins with acknowledgment
not a vague expression of regret
but a specific recognition of what was done
why it was harmful
and what the consequences have been for the other person

pronunciationNotes: acknowledgment: /əkˈnɑːlɪdʒmənt/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "악날리지먼트"에 가깝다. k 다음의 n을 분리하지 않는다.
consequences: /ˈkɑːnsɪkwensɪz/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "칸시퀀시즈"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: Without that specificity, an apology can feel like a performance designed to move past the incident rather than to address it honestly.
sentences.translation: 그러한 구체성이 없으면, 사과는 사건을 솔직하게 다루기 위한 것이 아니라 사건을 넘어가기 위해 설계된 연기처럼 느껴질 수 있다.

grammarElements.text: a performance designed to move past the incident
grammarElements.label: 보어 (과거분사 수식)

grammarElements.text: rather than to address it honestly
grammarElements.label: rather than 대조구

vocabulary.example: The apology lacked specificity.
The apology felt more like a performance than genuine regret.
We need to discuss the incident openly.

expressions.usage: 어떤 행위가 진심이 아니라 보여주기식임을 비유적으로 표현할 때 쓴다
문제를 제대로 해결하지 않고 지나가려 한다는 뉘앙스

patterns.pattern: Without [that quality], [subject] can feel like [metaphor] designed to [A] rather than to [B]
patterns.explanation: 핵심 요소가 빠졌을 때 부정적 결과가 생긴다는 것을 비유와 대조로 설명하는 패턴.
examples: Without sincerity, a compliment can feel like flattery designed to manipulate rather than to appreciate.
Without context, feedback can feel like criticism designed to belittle rather than to improve.

drillQuestions.question: Without that specificity, an apology can feel like a _____.
The apology is designed to move _____ the incident.
"Rather than to address it honestly" provides a _____ to the first purpose.
"Specificity" means being _____ and detailed.

sentences.notes: "Without ~" 전치사구가 조건을 제시하고, "a performance designed to ~"라는 비유가 형식적 사과의 공허함을 날카롭게 비판한다. "rather than to address it honestly"의 대조가 핵심이다.

koreanAlignment.en: Without that specificity
an apology can feel like a performance
designed to move past the incident
rather than to address it honestly
koreanAlignment.ko: 사건을 솔직하게 다루기 위한 것이 아니라

pronunciationNotes: specificity: /ˌspesɪˈfɪsəti/ — 세 번째 음절에 강세. "스페시피서티"에 가깝다.
performance: /pərˈfɔːrməns/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "퍼폼먼스"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: The next step is consistent behavior over time, because trust is rebuilt through patterns, not through single gestures.
sentences.translation: 다음 단계는 시간에 걸친 일관된 행동인데, 신뢰는 단일 행위가 아니라 패턴을 통해 재건되기 때문이다.

grammarElements.text: because trust is rebuilt through patterns, not through single gestures
grammarElements.label: because 이유절 (수동태 + 대조)

vocabulary.example: Consistent effort is more important than occasional brilliance.
Trust grows through repeated patterns of reliability.
A single grand gesture cannot replace daily kindness.

expressions.usage: 꾸준함이 핵심이라는 점을 강조하는 표현
한 번의 큰 행동으로는 부족하다는 뜻

patterns.pattern: The next step is [noun phrase], because [subject] is [rebuilt/achieved] through [A], not through [B]
patterns.explanation: 단계적 과정의 다음 단계를 제시하고, because절로 그 이유를 "A를 통해, B를 통해서가 아니라"라는 대조로 설명하는 패턴.
examples: The next step is daily practice, because fluency is achieved through repetition, not through memorization.
The next step is open dialogue, because understanding is built through conversation, not through assumptions.

drillQuestions.question: Trust is rebuilt through _____, not through single gestures.
"Consistent behavior over time" means behavior that is _____.
"The next step is" introduces the _____ stage in a process.
"Single gestures" means _____ actions.

sentences.notes: 단계적 논증의 전환을 "The next step is"로 명시한다. "through patterns, not through single gestures"의 대조가 과정 중심의 회복을 강조한다. 간결하면서도 핵심을 정확히 전달하는 문장이다.

koreanAlignment.en: The next step is
consistent behavior over time
because trust is rebuilt through patterns
not through single gestures

pronunciationNotes: consistent: /kənˈsɪstənt/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "컨시스턴트"에 가깝다.
gestures: /ˈdʒestʃərz/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "제스처즈"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: A grand apology followed by unchanged behavior is worse than no apology at all, because it adds the wound of broken promises to the original harm.
sentences.translation: 변하지 않은 행동이 뒤따르는 거창한 사과는 사과를 아예 하지 않는 것보다 더 나쁜데, 원래의 피해에 깨진 약속의 상처를 더하기 때문이다.

grammarElements.text: A grand apology followed by unchanged behavior
grammarElements.label: 주어 (과거분사 수식 포함)

grammarElements.text: worse than no apology at all
grammarElements.label: 보어 (비교급)

grammarElements.text: because it adds the wound of broken promises to the original harm
grammarElements.label: because 이유절

vocabulary.example: A grand gesture means nothing without follow-through.
His behavior remained unchanged despite the warning.
Broken promises leave deep emotional wounds.

expressions.usage: 과거분사(followed)가 앞의 명사를 수식하는 간결한 표현
기존 피해 위에 새로운 피해가 쌓인다는 뜻

patterns.pattern: [Grand action] followed by [unchanged behavior] is worse than [no action at all], because it adds [new harm] to [original harm]
patterns.explanation: 형식적 행동이 오히려 상황을 악화시키는 역설을 비교급과 because절로 설명하는 패턴.
examples: A lavish gift followed by repeated neglect is worse than no gift at all, because it adds the sting of false hope to ongoing loneliness.
A public pledge followed by no action is worse than silence, because it adds the damage of hypocrisy to the original problem.

drillQuestions.question: A grand apology followed by unchanged behavior is _____ than no apology at all.
It adds the wound of broken _____ to the original harm.
"Followed by" means coming _____ something.
"No apology at all" means _____ apologizing.

sentences.notes: "A grand apology followed by unchanged behavior"라는 긴 주어가 핵심 역설을 포착한다. 비교급(worse than)으로 형식적 사과의 해악을 강조하고, "the wound of broken promises"라는 은유가 감정적 무게를 더한다.

koreanAlignment.en: A grand apology followed by unchanged behavior
koreanAlignment.ko: 변하지 않은 행동이 뒤따르는 거창한 사과는

koreanAlignment.en: is worse than no apology at all
koreanAlignment.ko: 사과를 아예 하지 않는 것보다 더 나쁜데

koreanAlignment.en: because it adds the wound of broken promises
to the original harm

pronunciationNotes: wound: /wuːnd/ — 명사로 "상처"일 때는 /wuːnd/. 동사 wind의 과거형일 때는 /waʊnd/로 다르다.
unchanged: /ʌnˈtʃeɪndʒd/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. un- 접두사가 "변하지 않은"을 뜻한다.

sentences.original: Conceding fault is particularly difficult in organizational settings, where admitting error can carry legal or reputational risks, yet studies consistently show that institutions that acknowledge mistakes transparently recover public trust faster than those that deny or deflect.
sentences.translation: 잘못을 인정하는 것은 조직 환경에서 특히 어려운데, 오류를 인정하는 것이 법적 또는 평판 위험을 수반할 수 있기 때문이다. 그러나 연구들은 실수를 투명하게 인정하는 기관이 부인하거나 책임을 회피하는 기관보다 공적 신뢰를 더 빨리 회복한다는 것을 일관되게 보여준다.

grammarElements.text: where admitting error can carry legal or reputational risks
grammarElements.label: 관계부사절 (where)

grammarElements.text: yet studies consistently show that institutions that acknowledge mistakes transparently recover public trust faster than those that deny or deflect
grammarElements.label: yet 양보절 (이중 관계절 + 비교급)

vocabulary.example: Conceding fault is the first step toward repair.
Organizational culture affects how mistakes are handled.
The scandal posed a serious reputational risk.
The company tried to deflect blame onto its suppliers.
The organization acted transparently throughout the crisis.

expressions.usage: 공식적인 맥락에서 오류를 받아들이는 행위를 가리키는 격식적 표현
문제를 직면하지 않는 두 가지 회피 전략을 병렬로 제시하는 표현

patterns.pattern: [Gerund] is particularly difficult in [context], where [risk], yet studies show that [positive outcome for those who act] faster than [those who do not]
patterns.explanation: 어려움의 맥락을 먼저 인정하고, yet으로 전환하여 그럼에도 행동하는 것이 더 나은 결과를 낳는다는 연구 결과를 대비시키는 패턴. 매우 긴 복합문 구조이다.
examples: Admitting mistakes is particularly difficult in politics, where vulnerability invites attack, yet research shows that leaders who acknowledge errors retain more public support than those who cover up.
Sharing failures is particularly difficult in academia, where reputation drives funding, yet evidence shows that transparent researchers build stronger collaborations than those who hide setbacks.

drillQuestions.question: Conceding fault is particularly difficult in _____ settings.
Institutions that acknowledge mistakes _____ recover public trust faster.
"Deny or deflect" are strategies to _____ responsibility.
"Reputational risks" means risks to an organization's _____.

sentences.notes: 이 글에서 가장 긴 문장으로, 동명사 주어(Conceding fault), 관계부사절(where ~), yet 양보절, 이중 관계절(institutions that ~ vs. those that ~)이 모두 결합된 복합 구조이다. 조직 맥락으로 논의를 확장하는 핵심 문장이다.

koreanAlignment.en: Conceding fault is particularly difficult
in organizational settings
where admitting error can carry legal or reputational risks
koreanAlignment.ko: 오류를 인정하는 것이 법적 또는 평판 위험을 수반할 수 있기 때문이다

koreanAlignment.en: yet studies consistently show that
institutions that acknowledge mistakes transparently
recover public trust faster than those that deny or deflect
koreanAlignment.ko: 부인하거나 책임을 회피하는 기관보다 공적 신뢰를 더 빨리 회복한다는 것을

pronunciationNotes: organizational: /ˌɔːrɡənəˈzeɪʃənəl/ — 네 번째 음절에 강세. "오거너제이셔널"에 가깝다.
reputational: /ˌrepjəˈteɪʃənəl/ — 세 번째 음절에 강세. "레퓨테이셔널"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: The paradox is that the very act of admitting vulnerability — saying "we were wrong and here is what we are doing about it" — signals the kind of honesty that trust depends on.
sentences.translation: 역설은 취약성을 인정하는 바로 그 행위 — "우리가 잘못했고 이에 대해 이렇게 하고 있다"라고 말하는 것 — 가 신뢰가 의존하는 종류의 정직성을 보여준다는 것이다.

grammarElements.text: that the very act of admitting vulnerability
grammarElements.label: that 보어절의 주어 (강조 형용사 very)

grammarElements.text: saying "we were wrong and here is what we are doing about it"
grammarElements.label: 대시 삽입구 (동명사 동격)

grammarElements.text: signals the kind of honesty that trust depends on
grammarElements.label: 동사 + 목적어 (관계절 포함)

vocabulary.example: The paradox of choice is that more options lead to less satisfaction.
Showing vulnerability can strengthen relationships.
Her actions signal a genuine desire to change.

expressions.usage: very를 강조 형용사로 써서 "바로 그, 다름 아닌"이라는 뜻을 더한다

expressions.phrase: the kind of honesty that trust depends on
expressions.usage: 관계절로 특정한 종류의 자질을 명시하는 격식적 표현

patterns.pattern: The paradox is that the very act of [admitting weakness] — [concrete example] — signals [the quality needed]
patterns.explanation: 역설을 명시하고, 대시 삽입구로 구체적 예시를 넣은 뒤, 약함을 인정하는 것이 오히려 강함의 신호라는 논점을 전개하는 패턴.
examples: The paradox is that the very act of asking for help — saying "I do not know how to do this alone" — signals the kind of self-awareness that leadership requires.
The paradox is that the very act of slowing down — choosing fewer projects and deeper focus — signals the kind of discipline that productivity depends on.

drillQuestions.question: The paradox is that admitting vulnerability actually _____ honesty.
"The very act of" emphasizes that the act _____ is important.
"Trust depends on" means trust _____ something to exist.
"Vulnerability" means being open to _____.

sentences.notes: 글의 핵심 역설을 담은 문장. "the very act of admitting vulnerability"에서 very가 강조 형용사로 "바로 그 행위 자체"를 부각한다. 대시 삽입구로 구체적 발화 예시를 넣어 추상을 구체로 연결한다.

koreanAlignment.en: The paradox is that
the very act of admitting vulnerability
saying "we were wrong and here is what we are doing about it"
koreanAlignment.ko: "우리가 잘못했고 이에 대해 이렇게 하고 있다"라고 말하는 것

koreanAlignment.en: signals the kind of honesty
that trust depends on

pronunciationNotes: paradox: /ˈpærədɑːks/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "패러닥스"에 가깝다.
vulnerability: /ˌvʌlnərəˈbɪləti/ — 네 번째 음절에 강세. "벌너러빌러티"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: Rebuilding trust also requires patience from the injured party, who must be willing to observe new behavior long enough to determine whether it represents a genuine change or merely a temporary adjustment.
sentences.translation: 신뢰를 재건하는 것은 또한 피해를 입은 쪽의 인내심을 필요로 하는데, 그 사람은 새로운 행동이 진정한 변화를 나타내는 것인지 아니면 단지 일시적인 조정에 불과한 것인지 판단할 수 있을 만큼 충분히 오래 관찰할 의향이 있어야 한다.

grammarElements.text: who must be willing to observe new behavior long enough
grammarElements.label: 관계절 (must be willing to)

grammarElements.text: to determine whether it represents a genuine change or merely a temporary adjustment
grammarElements.label: to 부정사 목적절 (whether A or B)

vocabulary.example: Rebuilding trust requires enormous patience.
The injured party deserves time to heal.
Is this a genuine change or just an act?
The change was merely cosmetic.

expressions.usage: be willing to로 의향을 나타내고, observe로 주의 깊게 지켜보는 것을 표현

expressions.phrase: a genuine change or merely a temporary adjustment
expressions.meaning: 진정한 변화인지 아니면 단지 일시적인 조정인지
expressions.usage: whether A or B 구조로 두 가지 가능성을 제시하는 표현

patterns.pattern: [Gerund] also requires [quality] from [party], who must [verb] long enough to determine whether [A] or merely [B]
patterns.explanation: 상대편에게도 요구되는 자질을 명시하고, 관계절로 구체적 행동을 제시한 뒤 whether A or B로 판단 기준을 제시하는 패턴.
examples: Forgiving also requires courage from the victim, who must be willing to risk trust long enough to determine whether the change is real or merely performative.
Healing also requires openness from the patient, who must be willing to try new treatments long enough to determine whether they help or merely mask symptoms.

drillQuestions.question: Rebuilding trust also requires _____ from the injured party.
The injured party must observe new behavior long enough to _____ whether it is genuine.
"Merely a temporary adjustment" means a change that is _____.
"Who must be willing to" introduces a _____ on the injured party.

sentences.notes: 논의의 균형을 위해 피해자에게도 역할이 있다는 점을 강조하는 문장. 관계절(who must be willing to ~)이 길어 문장 구조가 복잡하다. whether A or merely B가 판단의 기준을 제시한다.

koreanAlignment.en: Rebuilding trust also requires patience
koreanAlignment.ko: 신뢰를 재건하는 것은 또한 인내심을 필요로 하는데

koreanAlignment.en: from the injured party
who must be willing to observe new behavior long enough
koreanAlignment.ko: 충분히 오래 새로운 행동을 관찰할 의향이 있어야 한다

koreanAlignment.en: to determine whether it represents a genuine change
koreanAlignment.ko: 진정한 변화를 나타내는 것인지 판단할 수 있을 만큼

koreanAlignment.en: or merely a temporary adjustment
koreanAlignment.ko: 아니면 단지 일시적인 조정에 불과한 것인지

pronunciationNotes: patience: /ˈpeɪʃəns/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "페이션스"에 가깝다. patient와 같은 강세 패턴.
genuine: /ˈdʒenjuɪn/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "제뉴인"에 가깝다. 마지막 e는 발음하지 않는다.

sentences.original: Neither side can rush the process, because trust restored too quickly is fragile, while trust rebuilt gradually through demonstrated reliability becomes, in many cases, even stronger than what existed before.
sentences.translation: 어느 쪽도 과정을 서두를 수 없는데, 너무 빨리 회복된 신뢰는 취약한 반면, 입증된 신뢰성을 통해 점진적으로 재건된 신뢰는 많은 경우 이전에 존재했던 것보다 더 강해지기 때문이다.

grammarElements.text: because trust restored too quickly is fragile
grammarElements.label: because 이유절 1 (과거분사 수식)

grammarElements.text: while trust rebuilt gradually through demonstrated reliability becomes, in many cases, even stronger than what existed before
grammarElements.label: while 대조절 (과거분사 수식 + 비교급 + 관계절)

vocabulary.example: You cannot rush the healing process.
Trust that is rebuilt too fast is fragile.
Trust was gradually rebuilt over many months.
His demonstrated reliability earned him the promotion.

expressions.usage: neither로 양쪽 모두를 부정하며 과정의 필연적 느림을 강조한다

expressions.phrase: even stronger than what existed before
expressions.usage: even이 비교급을 강조하며 놀라운 긍정적 결과를 나타낸다

patterns.pattern: Neither side can rush [the process], because [quick version] is [negative], while [slow version] becomes [positive] than [what came before]
patterns.explanation: 양측 모두에 해당되는 원칙을 선언하고, because와 while로 빠른 방식과 느린 방식의 결과를 대비하며, 점진적 접근의 우월함을 결론으로 제시하는 패턴.
examples: Neither party can rush reconciliation, because forgiveness granted too quickly is shallow, while forgiveness earned gradually through changed behavior becomes a deeper bond than the original.
Neither teacher nor student can rush mastery, because knowledge gained too quickly is superficial, while knowledge built steadily through practice becomes more durable than memorized facts.

drillQuestions.question: Trust restored too quickly is _____.
Trust rebuilt gradually becomes even _____ than before.
"Neither side" means _____ of the two parties.
"Demonstrated reliability" means reliability that has been _____.

sentences.notes: 글의 결론으로, "neither side"로 양쪽 모두에게 메시지를 전하며, while절로 빠른 회복과 느린 회복을 대비한다. "even stronger than what existed before"가 희망적 마무리를 제공하며, 과거분사 수식(trust restored / trust rebuilt)이 두 번 반복되는 고급 구조이다.

koreanAlignment.en: Neither side can rush the process
koreanAlignment.ko: 어느 쪽도 과정을 서두를 수 없는데

koreanAlignment.en: because trust restored too quickly is fragile
koreanAlignment.ko: 너무 빨리 회복된 신뢰는 취약한 반면

koreanAlignment.en: while trust rebuilt gradually through demonstrated reliability
koreanAlignment.ko: 입증된 신뢰성을 통해 점진적으로 재건된 신뢰는

koreanAlignment.en: becomes, in many cases, even stronger
than what existed before

pronunciationNotes: fragile: /ˈfrædʒəl/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "프래절"에 가깝다. 미국식에서 -ile은 /əl/로 약하게 발음.
reliability: /rɪˌlaɪəˈbɪləti/ — 네 번째 음절에 강세. "릴라이어빌러티"에 가깝다.
